pages.full_text:
11 stones supporting a table, making a chamber. A long entry chamber was destroyed 100 years ago to build a barn (now for sale) next to it. Built 4000 years ago + we've been married 40 years.
     For 50 years I have muddled Karnak (I saw in Syria or Jordan, tall columns) with Carnac here in France. Simon has pointed out the K. There is also a Krac here.
     On to the Dolmen of Mané-Groch. This one had side-chambers. Just long enough to sleep in ? or store bones? An entry passage, central area (10 + 10ft) + 4 side chambers off. Roof had fallen in on central one?
     Same size chambers as Grimes Graves, Norfolk. It's raining such a pity otherwise we could have biked to these little sites.
     Jesus on the way side + church stone crosses always has straight legs + toes crossed over each other.
     It rained more so we stopped at a Super U + shopped. Hair stuff good, slides, etc.
pages.notes: Text continues from previous spread — the opening '11 stones supporting a table' picks up mid-description of a megalithic monument, likely near Carnac, Brittany. 'Karnak' refers to the Egyptian/Middle-Eastern site; 'Carnac' is the Breton megalith site. 'Krac' likely refers to Krak des Chevaliers (Syria). 'Mané-Groch' is a dolmen near Carnac, Brittany. 'Grimes Graves' is a Neolithic flint mine in Norfolk, England. 'Super U' is a French supermarket chain. Year cannot be determined from this page alone; volume B covers 2003–2019. A decorative yellow wheat/grain motif appears near the bottom.

pages.full_text: St. Thégonnec, Renaissance Ossuary Chapel + entombment in oak in crypt.
photo_captions: St. Thégonnec, Renaissance Ossuary Chapel + entombment in oak in crypt.
pages.notes: Right page is dominated by a glued-in colour photograph of the Renaissance ossuary chapel at St-Thégonnec, Brittany, France. The only handwritten text is the caption below the photo. Handwritten page number '39' visible in top-right corner. A decorative yellow wheat/grain motif appears at the bottom. Text visible at the left edge of this image is the continuation from the left page (gutter overlap) and has not been re-transcribed here.
